original_hypothesis.title: Claimable helper decodes calldata directly and forwards to PhiFactory, enabling malformed calldata griefing and unexpected revert propagation in integrators
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Claimable.signatureClaim and Claimable.merkleClaim decode raw msg.data[4:] with strict length expectations (e.g., _decodeMerkleClaimData requires msg.data.length >= 260) and forward to PhiFactory. Any integration that exposes these helper functions (e.g., via PhiNFT1155 inheriting Claimable) can be griefed by sending malformed calldata that reverts with custom errors, potentially blocking batch relayers or meta-tx systems that assume standard ABI encoding and error formats.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is not a direct fund theft vector, but it can create operational instability for relayer/aggregator flows that route through Claimable helpers instead of calling PhiFactory directly.
deployment_assumptions: Relayers/aggregators call PhiNFT1155.signatureClaim()/merkleClaim() (from Claimable) as the public mint interface
Relayer batches multiple calls and fails whole batch on single revert
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No explicit deployment evidence; Claimable is inherited by PhiNFT1155 and exposes external payable signatureClaim()/merkleClaim().

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.decode(msg.data[4:], ...) with manual length check only for merkle; forwards msg.value to PhiFactory

preconditions: An operational component routes claims through Claimable functions on PhiNFT1155
Attacker can submit malformed calldata to that component

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Non-standard calldata parsing increases fragility; errors differ from PhiFactory direct calls

operational_questions: Are Claimable endpoints part of the public API used by relayers (Decent, etc.)?
Do relayers retry/fallback on revert, or do they fail batches and block other users?
validation_requirements: Trace a relayer-style batch including one malformed Claimable call to see if whole batch fails
Compare revert selectors and handling across integration stack

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit malformed calldata to PhiNFT1155.merkleClaim() (Claimable) that fails length/abi.decode
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Cause relayer batch to revert, delaying other users' claims

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Relayer/aggregator batch failures; degraded minting availability

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High revert rate on Claimable selectors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Relayer downtime correlated with malformed txs
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated low-cost revert spam targeting relayer endpoints

validation_result.mechanism: standard ABI decode revert on malformed input
validation_result.impact_summary: Not a code defect. Malformed calldata reverts are standard behavior for all Solidity functions regardless of decoding method.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that Claimable.signatureClaim() and Claimable.merkleClaim() use non-standard calldata decoding (abi.decode(msg.data[4:], ...)) that creates a griefing vector through malformed calldata. However, this behavior is functionally identical to standard ABI parameter decoding: if calldata doesn't match the expected types and lengths, the transaction reverts. This is true for EVERY Solidity function, not just these helpers. The manual abi.decode pattern is a design choice (the functions declare no formal parameters) but produces the same revert behavior as standard parameter declarations. The merkleClaim() has an additional msg.data.length < 260 check which provides earlier, cleaner error handling than raw abi.decode would. A relayer batch failing because one transaction contains malformed calldata is standard EVM behavior, not a code defect in these specific functions.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Claimable.sol signatureClaim() at lines ~20-43 and merkleClaim() at lines ~46-62 use abi.decode(msg.data[4:], ...) which reverts on malformed input. This is identical behavior to declaring formal parameters. _decodeMerkleClaimData() at lines ~64-82 adds a length check (msg.data.length < 260) that provides BETTER error handling than standard ABI decoding. No code defect exists.

calling_sequence: 1. External call to PhiNFT1155 (which inherits Claimable) with function selector for signatureClaim()
2. abi.decode(msg.data[4:], (bytes32, bytes32, address, address, address, uint256, uint256, uint256, string, bytes32)) - reverts if calldata malformed
3. If decode succeeds, constructs proper arguments and forwards to PhiFactory.signatureClaim()
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: No vulnerable code path exists. The abi.decode revert on malformed input is standard defensive behavior identical to what any Solidity function with typed parameters would exhibit. Sending garbage calldata to any function on any contract causes a revert.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Relayers/aggregators call PhiNFT1155.signatureClaim()/merkleClaim() as the public mint interface
Relayer batches multiple calls and fails whole batch on single revert
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (Deploy.s.sol, DeployWoCred.s.sol) show no relayer configuration, no batching infrastructure, and no references to using Claimable endpoints as the primary mint interface. The primary claim flow appears to be through PhiFactory.claim() and PhiFactory.batchClaim().

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario posits that an attacker sends malformed calldata to PhiNFT1155.merkleClaim() to cause a revert that disrupts a relayer batch. However: (1) This is not specific to the Claimable implementation - ANY function call with malformed data reverts. (2) The merkleClaim's _decodeMerkleClaimData() actually provides BETTER error handling with its explicit length check (msg.data.length < 260) compared to standard ABI decoding which would give an opaque revert. (3) Relayer/batch systems must handle individual transaction reverts as a fundamental design requirement. (4) The primary claim interface appears to be PhiFactory.claim()/batchClaim(), not the Claimable helpers. This is not a code defect but standard EVM behavior.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No real impact. The described behavior (revert on malformed calldata) is identical to what occurs with any Solidity function receiving invalid input. The non-standard function signature pattern (no declared parameters, manual msg.data decoding) is a design choice that doesn't introduce additional risk.
validation_result.replication_plan: N/A - no defect to replicate. The test would simply confirm that malformed calldata causes a revert, which is standard behavior.
uncertainties: Whether any production relayer or aggregator uses the Claimable helper functions on PhiNFT1155 as the primary mint endpoint (unlikely given PhiFactory.claim() exists)
